text: This article examines the transformative impact of the Circle of Concerned African Women Theologians on the landscape of higher theological education across the African continent. Historically, theological academia in Africa has been characterized by significant gender disparities, shaped by the overlapping influences of colonialism, race, and class. In response, the Circle emerged as a vital Pan-African movement dedicated to dismantling these patriarchal structures and fostering an environment of inclusivity and intersectional solidarity. Utilizing African Women’s Theology as a theoretical framework and employing a qualitative methodological approach, the study analyzes the organization's efforts to integrate gender-sensitive curricula and provide essential mentorship for emerging female scholars. The findings highlight the Circle's historical journey and its enduring contributions to promoting gender equality within African theological discourse, ultimately demonstrating how the organization has successfully cultivated a new generation of African women theologians and reshaped the academic study of religion.
